title: Patty melt
subtitle: Beef patty with caramelised onions and melted gruyère on rye
excerpt: A diner classic: a thin beef patty with caramelised onions and gruyère pressed between two slices of dark rye and cooked on a buttered griddle until the bread is bronze and the cheese runs into the meat. Halfway between a burger and a toasted sandwich.
sourceNotes: The patty melt appears in American diner culture from the 1940s onward, associated with short-order kitchens serving both burgers and grilled sandwiches on the same flat-top. It combines the griddled beef patty with the cheese-toast method of the Reuben and club sandwich traditions. Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) documents the American minced beef canon that underpins the dish.

content.text: A patty melt is what happens when a diner cook runs out of burger buns and reaches for the rye bread instead. The beef patty is formed flat rather than rounded (it needs to cover the slice of bread) and cooked on a buttered flat-top alongside caramelised onions that have gone jammy and sweet over a low heat. Gruyère melts into the meat and bread as the whole thing is pressed together on the griddle.

The onions take the most time. Give them 20 minutes over a medium-low heat in a generous knob of butter, stirring occasionally, until they are deep gold and sweet. The patty itself cooks in 3 minutes per side, no fussing. Press the assembled sandwich firmly on the griddle so the bread makes full contact with the butter and browns evenly.

items.name: Beef mince
items.prepNote: 20% fat, formed into 2 flat patties the size of the bread slices

content.text: Melt 20 g of the butter in the skillet over medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} thinly sliced onions with a pinch of salt. Cook for 18 to 22 minutes, stirring every few minutes, until deep gold and collapsed. Transfer to a bowl.

Season the {{beef-mince}} patties with salt and black pepper. Raise the heat to medium-high and add a little of the remaining butter to the pan. Cook the patties for 3 minutes per side until cooked through and lightly crusted. Set aside.
Assemble and press on the griddle

Spread the remaining butter generously on one side of each slice of bread. Lay two slices butter-side down in the pan over medium heat. Spread a little mustard on the top face if using. Lay half the cheese on each, then a patty, then the caramelised onions, then the remaining cheese. Top with the remaining bread slices, butter-side up. Press firmly with the fish slice. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes per side, pressing occasionally, until the bread is dark golden and the cheese is fully melted.

items.fix: Cook on medium rather than medium-high; cover the pan loosely with foil for 1 minute after flipping to trap heat and melt the cheese
items.cause: Heat too high; rye bread colours faster than white bread
items.symptom: Bread is burning before cheese melts

items.fix: Keep the heat at medium-low and allow 20 minutes; add a pinch of salt early to draw out moisture
items.cause: Cooked too quickly over high heat; they need gentle, slow caramelisation
items.symptom: Onions are sharp rather than sweet

items.fix: Use 20% fat mince and cook no longer than 3 minutes per side; the patty will continue cooking when pressed with the sandwich
Apply steady pressure with the fish slice throughout cooking; the butter on the bread acts as a seal when it crisps
items.cause: Not pressed firmly enough during cooking

content.text: The patty melt is a diner standard with no particularly famous origin, which is part of what makes it representative. It emerged from the American flat-top tradition where the same griddle that cooked burgers also pressed cheese sandwiches, and the combination was the obvious next step. The rye bread is the deciding detail: it has enough density to hold up to the butter and the pressing without going soggy, and the caraway-inflected flavour works with the sweet onions in a way that white bread never quite matches. Serve with a pickle and something cold.
